—The psalmist decides to praise God himself. Our encouragement and our examples should be consistent. He suggests the works of the Lord as the proper subject when we are praising him; and his dealings in providence toward the world, the church, and individual people. All the works of the Lord are spoken of as one; it is his work; so wonderfully do all the dispensations of his providence center around one design. The works of God, carefully and diligently examined, will all be found just and holy. God’s forgiving of sin is the most wonderful of all his works, and ought to be remembered for his glory. He will always remember his covenant; he has always done so, and he always will do so. His works of providence were done according to the truth of the Divine promises and prophecies, and they are true; and by him who has a right to dispose of the earth as he pleases, and therefore are just. This holds good of the work of grace upon the heart of man, verse #( ). All God’s commandments are sure; all have been fulfilled by Christ, and remain with him as a rule for our walk and conversation. He sent redemption to his people out of Egypt at first, and often afterwards; and these were symbols of the great redemption which in the fullness of time was to be accomplished by the Lord Jesus. Here his everlasting righteousness shines forth along with his boundless mercy. No person is wise who does not fear the Lord; no person acts wisely except as influenced by that fear. This fear will lead to repentance, to faith in Christ, to watchfulness and obedience. Such people have a good understanding, however poor, unlearned, or despised.
